sources.title: Direct parse of references 24-30
facts: The six monograph files claim 182 textbook primary formulas and contain all 182 after recognizing both guillemets and parenthetical source-attribution formats.

Four primary formulas use parenthetical source attribution: Jiuwei Qianghuo Tang, Huanglian Jiedu Tang, Jiuxian San and Zixue. Four additional lines are explicitly marked classic anchors and remain separate, producing 186 total definitions.

sources.title: FDA 101: Dietary Supplements
facts: Herbal products can have strong effects, interactions and adverse events, and are not approved for safety and effectiveness before sale as dietary supplements.
Disease diagnosis, treatment, cure or prevention claims require the applicable drug evidence and regulation.

sources.title: Traditional Chinese Medicine: What You Need To Know
facts: Evidence for Chinese herbal products varies by intervention and condition and is often mixed or poor quality.
Traditional formula attribution does not validate a modern hypertension, cancer, stroke or emergency-treatment claim.

sources.title: Pharmacopoeia of the People's Republic of China, 2025 edition
facts: Current medicine identity, processing, dose and precautions require medicine-level authoritative comparison.
A blanket conversion from historical formula quantities to modern grams is not a validated dose contract.

sources.title: NMPA notices on Guanmutong and six aristolochic-acid medicines
facts: The medicinal standard for Qingmuxiang was cancelled in 2004 and replacement with Tumuxiang was specified.
Historical formula text that still names Qingmuxiang requires explicit identity and regulatory adjudication and remains blocked.

sources.title: Import Alert 54-10: Products Containing Aristolochic Acid
facts: Aristolochic acid is a potent carcinogen and nephrotoxin associated with irreversible renal damage and renal failure.
Name confusion and substitution are recognized routes of exposure.

sources.title: Regulation on Medical Toxic Drugs and current official reproductions
facts: The legal toxic-medicine control list includes high-risk arsenic, mercury, aconite and other Chinese medicines.
A formula composition or dose containing a controlled toxic medicine is not consumer self-treatment authority.

sources.title: FDA warning on heavy metal poisoning associated with traditional drug products
facts: Lead, mercury and arsenic exposure can cause serious kidney, neurologic, gastrointestinal and other injury.
Possible exposure or symptoms require professional or poison-center consultation.

sources.title: Poisoning first aid
facts: Do not induce vomiting unless directed by poison control or a health professional.
Do not use a cure-all antidote or attempt neutralization without professional direction.

sources.title: Signs and Symptoms of Stroke
facts: Sudden neurologic deficits require emergency response and immediate medical care.
A traditional closed-syndrome or formula lookup must not delay stroke evaluation.

sources.title: Gastrointestinal bleeding
facts: Vomiting blood, black stool and major gastrointestinal bleeding require urgent medical assessment.
A formula labeled for emergency hemostasis is not a first-aid or self-treatment protocol.

boundaries: These comparators establish source-integrity, dose, regulatory, poisoning, emergency and efficacy boundaries; they do not validate every formula composition or indication.
Complete source preservation and 186 formatted definition lines do not validate formula composition, dose, indication or all 182 claimed attached formulas as independent entities.
No formula, dose, route, scenario lookup, poisoning action, pregnancy use, emergency use or modern efficacy claim becomes runtime eligible in this layer.
